figures.caption: Fig. 1. Bleeding Artifacts in LiDAR Densification. An example of a densified result generated by the baseline method LiDPM. Note that without physical constraints, existing methods often generate severe ghost artifacts extending behind objects into free space.

Fig. 2. The proposed Scanline-Consistent Range-Aware Diffusion framework. (Left) Stage-0 constructs a coarse structural prior. (Middle) Stage-1 performs Partial Diffusion. The Probabilistic Range Head predicts both range noise and an uncertainty scale b̂. (Right) Physics-Aware Constraints. We employ an Isotropic Probabilistic Consistency loss. The penalty is weighted by a spatial Gaussian kernel (Lateral Weight) scaled by b̂, allowing the uncertainty to dynamically determine the valid geometric tolerance for both lateral misalignment (d⊥) and radial occlusion (d∥).

Fig. 3. Negative Ray Augmentation Strategy. (Panel A) On positive rays, the model learns a probabilistic range distribution. The uncertainty b̂ (visualized as the green confidence region) represents the isotropic spatial tolerance learned by the network. (Panel B) On negative rays sampled from known free space, we enforce a zero-occupancy constraint. This explicitly teaches the Occupancy Head to suppress ghost points in free space where the sensor beam should pass through unimpeded.

Fig. 4. Qualitative Comparison of Densification Results. LiDPM preserves local detail but introduces scattered outlier points near object edges and occasional ghost points in free space. Our method generates sharp structures with clean free space in the demonstration.
TABLE III IMPLEMENTATION DETAILS OF THE PROPOSED METHOD.

TABLE I QUANTITATIVE COMPARISON ON KITTI-360 AND NUSCENES VALIDATION SETS. ↓ INDICATES LOWER IS BETTER. BEST RESULTS ARE IN BOLD AND SECOND-BEST ARE UNDERLINED. PVD AND PCDM ARE CAPPED AT 8K POINTS DUE TO ATTENTION MEMORY LIMITS.
